slides.title: What is Linear Regression?
slides.content: Linear Regression models the relationship between a dependent variable (response) and one or more independent variables (features). It's used to predict outcomes based on continuous data.

slides.title: Intuition Behind Linear Regression
slides.content: Think of it as drawing a line of best fit through data points to summarize their relationship.

visual_assets.description: A scatter plot with a line of best fit illustrating the concept of Linear Regression.
visual_assets.caption: Visualizing Linear Regression

slides.title: Key Assumptions of Linear Regression
slides.content:
- Linearity
- Independence
- Homoscedasticity
- Normality
These assumptions ensure the validity of Linear Regression results.

slides.title: Objective: Minimizing Loss
slides.content:
The objective is to minimize the difference between observed values and the values predicted by the model.
Commonly, this is done by minimizing the Mean Squared Error.

slides.title: Fitting the Model
slides.content:
Fitting is finding the parameters (slope and intercept) that minimize the loss function.
Using analytical methods like the Normal Equation, or iterative methods such as Gradient Descent.

slides.title: Interpreting Results
slides.content:
Interpret the coefficients to understand the relationship:
- Slope: Change in response for a one-unit change in predictor.
- Intercept: Expected response when predictors are zero.

visual_assets.description: Table of coefficients with their interpretations.
visual_assets.caption: Interpreting Coefficients

slides.title: Evaluation Metrics
slides.content:
- R^2: Proportion of variance explained by the model.
- RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error): Measures model prediction error.
- MAE (Mean Absolute Error): Average absolute error between predicted and actual.
